mechanism: Participants observe videos of entities performing truthful and deceptive statements. Judgments of deception versus truth are coded for each video. Binary classification accuracy is computed per participant and analyzed by group membership and target entity group. Response time and eye-tracking fixation patterns to deception-related cues are recorded to measure latency and attention allocation. Mediation and moderation analyses examine whether motivation to avoid group-based inferences predicts response patterns and whether fixation timing precedes explicit judgment.
domain: intergroup social perception and deception detection

mechanism: Participants view images of entities with varying group membership (in-group versus out-group) and health status (healthy versus infected appearance). Memory confusion paradigm measures whether healthy out-group entities are more often confused with infected in-group entities than other combinations. Confusion rates are tabulated and compared across group and health dimensions. A signal-detection analysis examines whether confusion patterns reflect shared psychological category representation or independent coding.

Participants form successive impressions of two fictional groups, each with an equal number of positive and negative attributes. Preference judgments for each group are recorded after exposure. Analysis compares preference for the first-encountered group versus the novel group. Attribute uniqueness (whether attributes distinguish the two groups) is manipulated orthogonally. Evaluation differences are tested as a function of whether unique attributes are positive or negative.

Participants either perform unethical behaviors or morally neutral behaviors in experimental tasks. Self-reported humanness is measured via questionnaire on continuous scales. In a reverse manipulation, entities are induced to feel self-dehumanized via priming or feedback, then given opportunities to behave unethically. Binary choice of ethical versus unethical action is recorded. Longitudinal patterns are examined within individuals to assess whether dehumanization predicts subsequent unethical behavior.

Entities in groups decide whether to contribute costs to a common pool (cooperation versus free riding). Other entities evaluate and vote on sanctioning the free rider. Evaluation scores and sanction decisions are recorded and compared by age group and by whether noncontribution was intentional versus accidental. Effect size and robustness across third-party and first-party contexts are computed. Confounds (nonconformity, total cost to group) are controlled via statistical comparison of conditions.

A belief measure assesses whether entities hold fixed versus growth implicit theories about interests. Either this measure is used observationally or a belief is experimentally induced via scenario manipulation. Engagement with novel domains is measured via time spent or choice of difficulty when interests become challenging. Interest maintenance is tracked over repeated exposure. Effects are tested as binary comparisons between theory groups or as continuous correlations between theory strength and engagement metrics.
domain: motivation and implicit theories of interests

mechanism: Facial images of officials are presented to raters who assess traits on continuous scales. Official corruption status (convicted, clean records, or campaign violations) is the ground truth label. Correlations between trait judgments and corruption status are computed. A causal mechanism is tested by digitally manipulating a facial feature (width) while holding identity constant, then remeasuring trait judgments and correlation to manipulated feature values. Mediation analysis examines whether trait judgment (e.g., corruptibility inference) explains the observed correlation.

Entities observe a statistical scenario with two groups and base-rate information (group-level priors). They judge probability of an outcome given an individual's observed data. In a critical condition, prior information differs between groups, making Bayesian-optimal judgments unequal while equality is morally prescribed. The same entity simultaneously judges another entity's identical decision, rating its moral and intellectual quality. Willingness to share money with or punish the other entity is measured. Comparison tests whether the entity's own judgment violates equality while condemning equality violation in others.

Entities judge whether five-word sequences are grammatically correct or incorrect. Critical ungrammatical sequences are formed by transposing two adjacent words. The source sequence (grammatical or ungrammatical) is varied orthogonally. Reaction time and accuracy are recorded per trial. A comparison tests whether reaction time is longer and accuracy lower when the ungrammatical sequence originates from a grammatical base than from an ungrammatical base. Analysis of error patterns and position effects infers underlying word-order encoding mechanisms.

Entities report initial attitudes on a policy issue and the degree to which those attitudes are based on moral reasoning. A persuasive message with either moral or nonmoral framing is presented, and attitude change is measured post-message. Analysis tests whether the effect of message framing (moral versus nonmoral) on attitude change is moderated by the baseline moralization of the initial attitude. Mediation analysis examines whether valenced thoughts about the message explain the moderating effect.

Entities evaluate the credibility of a conclusion presented as endorsed by multiple sources. In a critical manipulation, the apparent consensus is either true (derived from independent primary sources) or false (all sources trace to a single primary source, then propagated). Confidence in the conclusion is measured on continuous scales, before and after entities are told the consensus basis. Analysis compares confidence levels between true and false consensus conditions and tests whether explicit knowledge of the distinction changes confidence ratings.

Entities view complex event scenarios with multiple elements (objects, locations, actions, and features). Recall is tested on each element independently. Dependency analysis computes the probability of recall success for one element conditional on success for another element from the same event, compared to null independence. The degree of dependence (pattern completion connectivity) is quantified for each age group. Differences in holistic binding strength are tested as a function of age.

Entities rate pseudowords on affective arousal (subjective scale or objective acoustic features extracted via signal processing) and shapes on visual properties (rounded versus spiky). Pseudowords are matched to shapes via explicit pairing tasks. Matching preferences are tabulated. A mediation analysis tests whether affective arousal of the pseudoword fully explains the matching bias to shape properties. The mediation is tested across novel pseudowords via a preregistered experiment.

Entities choose between two lotteries on some trials; on other trials, a lottery is assigned to them. For each lottery, curiosity is measured via self-report rating or willingness-to-wait duration before outcome revelation. A comparison tests whether curiosity is higher when the entity chose the lottery versus when it was assigned, controlling for initial preference via statistical covariance. Curiosity measures are treated as continuous outcomes.

Items assessing safety behaviors in high-stakes testing contexts are generated from interviews. An exploratory factor analysis reduces a 33-item pool to 19 items by examining item distributions and factor loadings. A confirmatory factor analysis validates the two-factor structure on a separate sample. Correlations between factors and academic performance are computed to test criterion validity. Internal consistency is assessed via Cronbach's alpha. Discriminant and convergent validity are examined via correlations with related measures.

Entities are scored on a continuous trait scale (callous-unemotional propensity) and separately assessed for maltreatment history across categories (sexual abuse, neglect, violent/property delinquency). Clustering or latent-class analysis identifies distinct multivariate profiles of trait and maltreatment combinations. Group membership is compared to concurrent psychopathology and behavioral delinquency measures. Differences in offense type (sexual motivation) distinguish profiles. Variance in aggression and drug delinquency across profiles versus low-trait entities is tested.

Entities are scored on a generalized trust scale (continuous, measuring default expectations of others' trustworthiness). Entities view videos of truthful and deceptive statements. Accuracy of lie detection is computed per entity (proportion correct). Correlation or regression analysis tests the relationship between trust score and detection accuracy. Analysis controls for video difficulty by standardizing performance relative to baseline.

Retracted article: original research designed experiments manipulating sensory modalities (vision, hearing, touching, tasting, smelling) at global versus local processing levels. Entities performed creative and analytic thinking tasks. Analysis compared performance across modality and processing-level conditions. The retraction indicates data manipulation in the control group variability, invalidating the claimed effects.

Mortality salience is induced via a standard priming manipulation. Death-related thought accessibility is measured via lexical decision or word-completion tasks, where response time or error rate to death-related versus control words is recorded. Measurement is repeated immediately after salience induction and after a delay period. A comparison tests the hypothesis that accessibility is higher at delay than immediately, predicting a suppression-and-rebound pattern.
domain: terror management theory and mortality cognition

mechanism: Entities observe descriptions of actors making moral or immoral decisions either quickly or slowly. Moral character evaluations are collected via continuous rating scales. A mediation chain is tested: decision speed is proposed to influence perceived certainty, which influences inferred motive clarity, which in turn explains moral character evaluation. Path analysis or process dissociation estimates direct and indirect effects. Conditions are compared (moral versus immoral decision; quick versus slow speed) via factorial ANOVA or regression.
